criteria.good: Every inter-part interface names a specific connection type (bolted, threaded_insert, snap_fit, press_fit, dovetail_slide, clevis_pin, revolute_bearing, band_clamp, adhesive_lap, o_ring, connector_mate, harness_passthrough) with its DOF and a numeric clearance.
criteria.bad: Interfaces are labeled only 'mount' or 'attach' with no connection type, DOF, or clearance.
criteria.loop_feedback: For each interface pick a connection type from the taxonomy, state its DOF (0/1R/1T/1R1T/3R), and add a numeric clearance and the matching geometry feature.

criteria.good: Parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable; no installed part blocks access to a later one; last-installed and captive parts are identified.
criteria.bad: Assembly order is unstated or impossible — a part traps another, or a fastener ends up behind a wall with no access.
criteria.loop_feedback: State an installation order and verify each joint is reachable when it is made; reorder or add access openings where a part would trap another.

criteria.good: One primary datum part anchors the assembly; each other part locates off a named parent datum using a 3-2-1 scheme, so tolerance does not accumulate uncontrolled across the stack.
criteria.bad: Parts float with no shared datum, or each part references a different feature so stack-up grows part-to-part.
criteria.loop_feedback: Name one primary datum part, route each part's location back to it through one parent datum, and keep critical fits off the primary datum to limit stack-up.

criteria.good: Every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground; the load path closes for all parts and high-load joints use load-appropriate connection types.
criteria.bad: One or more parts carry load with no continuous path to ground, or a high-load joint uses a low-load connection (e.g. snap-fit holding a structural member).
criteria.loop_feedback: Trace each part's main load to the base through named interfaces; upgrade any load-bearing joint from snap/adhesive to bolted/pinned/clamped and add the fitting where the path breaks.

criteria.good: Compared to the previous version the design moves the right direction: fewer interfaces, parts consolidated where service is preserved, open load paths closed, and redundancy added only at critical joints.
criteria.bad: Iteration adds interfaces or loose parts without justification, or consolidates in a way that buries service access.
criteria.loop_feedback: Drive the next version toward fewer justified interfaces, consolidated printable parts that keep service access, and closed load paths; add redundancy only at safety-critical joints.

criteria.good: The assembly uses the fewest interfaces that still allow service and manufacture; each remaining interface is justified by service, motion, manufacture split, or load transfer.
criteria.bad: Many redundant or unexplained interfaces, or arbitrary part splits that add joints without a service/manufacture reason.
criteria.loop_feedback: Merge interfaces that exist for no service/motion/manufacture/load reason; keep a split only when it earns its joint.
